title: The Night Market
summary: A narrator describes her first visit to a night market in a Southeast Asian city — the overwhelming sensory experience, the food she discovers, and the feeling of being a welcome stranger in an unfamiliar world.
body: Nobody had warned me quite how loud it would be. I had stepped off a crowded bus into a narrow street, and suddenly the night market was all around me — a wall of sound, colour, and heat that hit me before I had time to prepare for it. Strings of bare light bulbs hung between the stalls, casting a warm yellow glow over everything. Generators hummed at the edges. Vendors called out to passing customers in a language I did not understand, but the friendliness in their voices was unmistakable. The smell was the first thing to really stop me in my tracks. Somewhere nearby, meat was being grilled over charcoal, and the smoke drifted through the air in slow, fragrant waves. Mixed in with it were the sharp scents of fresh herbs, the vinegary tang of a sauce I could not identify, and beneath all of that, something sweet — grilled banana, I later discovered. I had been travelling alone for three weeks by that point, and I had grown accustomed to navigating unfamiliar places. But nothing had quite prepared me for the density of this place — every square metre filled with something to look at, eat, or hear. I worked my way slowly through the crowd, pausing at each stall to see what was on offer. A woman was assembling bowls of noodle soup with extraordinary speed, ladling broth from an enormous pot. An old man sat cross-legged on a low plastic stool, frying something that sizzled loudly in a wok. A teenage girl with a shy smile held out a skewer of fruit for me to try. I hesitated for a moment — not because I was afraid, but because I wanted to hold on to that particular feeling: of being handed something by a stranger in a place you do not belong, and knowing that you are welcome anyway. The fruit was lychee, slightly warm from the grill. It was unlike anything I had eaten before. I ended up spending four hours at that market without intending to. I tried things I could not name, pointed at dishes and hoped for the best, and once laughed so hard at a misunderstanding with a vendor that we both ended up in tears. By the time I finally found my way back to the main road, I was full, tired, and oddly moved. Travel has a strange power: it can make you feel utterly lost and completely at home at the same time. That night market did both, and I was grateful for every overwhelming, glorious minute of it.

annotations.meaningVi: khiến tôi dừng lại đột ngột
annotations.exampleEn: The sudden noise stopped her in her tracks.
The garden party was lit with strings of bare light bulbs.
The fireplace was casting a warm glow across the room.

annotations.meaningVi: người bán hàng rong, tiểu thương
annotations.exampleEn: Street vendors sell food and souvenirs near the entrance.

annotations.meaningVi: không thể nhầm lẫn được, rõ ràng
annotations.exampleEn: There was an unmistakable smell of fresh coffee.
The sound of music drifted through the open window.

annotations.meaningVi: thơm ngát, có hương thơm dễ chịu
annotations.exampleEn: The market was filled with the fragrant smell of spices.
The dressing had a pleasant vinegary tang.

annotations.meaningVi: đã quen với, đã thích nghi với
annotations.exampleEn: She had grown accustomed to living alone.
The density of people in the market was overwhelming.
She was ladling soup into bowls for the guests.
He hesitated before knocking on the door.
I wanted to hold on to that happy feeling.

annotations.meaningVi: hy vọng mọi thứ sẽ ổn
annotations.exampleEn: We didn't have a plan — we just went hoping for the best.

annotations.meaningVi: xúc động một cách kỳ lạ
annotations.exampleEn: She was oddly moved by the simple kindness of strangers.
We had a glorious day at the beach.
The audience was in tears by the end of the film.

grammarNotes.title: Past Perfect and Past Simple — Sequencing events in a narrative
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài văn trộn lẫn quá khứ đơn (hành động chính trong câu chuyện) với quá khứ hoàn thành (hành động xảy ra trước đó). 'I had stepped off a crowded bus' và 'I had been travelling alone for three weeks by that point' đặt những hành động này trước thời điểm trung tâm của câu chuyện. Quá khứ hoàn thành tạo ra chiều sâu thời gian trong văn tự sự.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage mixes past simple (main narrative actions) with past perfect (actions prior to the narrative moment). 'I had stepped off a crowded bus' and 'I had been travelling alone for three weeks by that point' anchor these actions before the central narrative moment. The past perfect creates temporal depth in storytelling.

grammarNotes.title: Gerund Phrases as Subjects and Objects
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài dùng danh động từ (gerund) ở nhiều vị trí để diễn đạt các ý phức tạp. 'Being handed something by a stranger' là chủ ngữ của câu; 'navigating unfamiliar places' là tân ngữ của 'accustomed to'. Gerund (V-ing dùng như danh từ) cho phép diễn đạt hành động trừu tượng một cách tự nhiên và lưu loát.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses gerund phrases in various positions. 'Being handed something by a stranger' functions as the subject of a clause; 'navigating unfamiliar places' is the object of 'accustomed to'. Gerunds (V-ing used as nouns) allow abstract actions to be expressed naturally and fluidly in English.

translationVi:
Không ai cảnh báo tôi về việc nó ồn đến mức nào. Tôi bước xuống từ một chiếc xe buýt đông đúc ra một con phố hẹp, và đột nhiên khu chợ đêm ở khắp xung quanh tôi — một bức tường âm thanh, màu sắc, và nhiệt đến trước khi tôi kịp chuẩn bị.

Những dây bóng đèn trần treo giữa các gian hàng, tỏa ra ánh vàng ấm áp trên mọi thứ. Máy phát điện kêu vo ve ở các rìa. Người bán hàng gọi mời khách đi ngang qua bằng một ngôn ngữ tôi không hiểu, nhưng sự thân thiện trong giọng họ rõ ràng không thể nhầm lẫn.

Mùi hương là thứ đầu tiên thực sự khiến tôi dừng lại. Đâu đó gần đó, thịt đang được nướng trên than hồng, và khói toả ra trong không khí trong những làn chậm, thơm ngát. Hoà lẫn vào đó là mùi sắc nét của rau thơm tươi, vị chua thanh của một loại nước sốt tôi không nhận ra, và bên dưới tất cả, một thứ gì đó ngọt ngào — chuối nướng, tôi phát hiện ra sau đó.

Lúc đó tôi đã đi du lịch một mình được ba tuần, và tôi đã quen với việc tìm đường ở những nơi xa lạ. Nhưng không có gì thực sự chuẩn bị tôi cho sự dày đặc của nơi này — mỗi mét vuông lấp đầy thứ gì đó để nhìn, ăn, hoặc nghe.

Tôi len lỏi chậm rãi qua đám đông, dừng lại ở mỗi gian hàng để xem có gì. Một người phụ nữ đang lắp ráp từng bát mì với tốc độ đáng kinh ngạc, múc nước dùng từ một nồi khổng lồ. Một cụ ông ngồi xếp bằng trên một chiếc ghế nhựa thấp, chiên thứ gì đó kêu xèo xèo trong chảo. Một cô gái tuổi teen với nụ cười nhút nhát chìa ra một que trái cây để tôi thử.

Tôi do dự một lúc — không phải vì tôi sợ, mà vì tôi muốn giữ lấy cảm giác đặc biệt đó: được một người lạ trao thứ gì đó ở một nơi bạn không thuộc về, và biết rằng bạn vẫn được chào đón.

Món trái cây là vải, hơi ấm từ vỉ nướng. Nó khác hẳn bất cứ thứ gì tôi từng ăn. Cuối cùng tôi đã ở lại chợ đó bốn tiếng mà không có ý định như vậy. Tôi thử những thứ tôi không biết tên, chỉ vào các món ăn và hy vọng mọi chuyện ổn, và một lần cười đến chảy nước mắt vì hiểu lầm với một người bán hàng đến mức cả hai cùng rơi lệ.

Khi tôi cuối cùng tìm được đường trở ra đường lớn, tôi đã no, mệt, và kỳ lạ thay, xúc động. Du lịch có một sức mạnh kỳ lạ: nó có thể khiến bạn cảm thấy hoàn toàn lạc lõng và hoàn toàn ở nhà cùng một lúc. Khu chợ đêm đó đã làm cả hai điều, và tôi biết ơn từng phút tuyệt vời, choáng ngợp đó.

questions.prompt: How does the narrator describe first entering the night market?
options: She walked in slowly, taking time to look around
She was hit by a wall of sound, colour, and heat before she was ready
She felt calm and prepared because she had visited markets before
She was immediately welcomed by a vendor who spoke English
questions.explanation: The narrator describes 'a wall of sound, colour, and heat that hit me before I had time to prepare for it.'
questions.hint: The narrator uses a vivid metaphor for her first impression.

questions.prompt: What created the lighting at the night market?
options: Colourful lanterns hanging from the stalls
Strings of bare light bulbs hung between the stalls
questions.explanation: The narrator describes 'Strings of bare light bulbs hung between the stalls, casting a warm yellow glow over everything.'
questions.hint: The narrator specifically describes what kind of lights were used.

questions.prompt: What was the first thing that really stopped the narrator in her tracks?
options: The sound of a vendor calling out to her
The sight of an enormous cooking pot
The smell of meat being grilled over charcoal
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'The smell was the first thing to really stop me in my tracks.' She then describes meat being grilled over charcoal.
questions.hint: The narrator says one sense stopped her above all others.

questions.prompt: What sweet smell did the narrator eventually identify?
options: Caramelised sugar from a candy stall
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'something sweet — grilled banana, I later discovered.'
questions.hint: She mentions the sweet smell last but identifies it later.

questions.prompt: How long had the narrator been travelling alone when she visited the night market?
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'I had been travelling alone for three weeks by that point.'
questions.hint: She mentions this when describing her experience with unfamiliar places.

questions.prompt: Why did the narrator hesitate when the teenage girl offered her fruit to try?
options: She was worried the fruit might not be safe to eat
She did not want to spend any money
She wanted to hold on to the feeling of the moment
She was allergic to tropical fruit
questions.explanation: The narrator explains: 'not because I was afraid, but because I wanted to hold on to that particular feeling.'
questions.hint: She explicitly explains her reason for hesitating.

questions.prompt: What was the fruit the teenage girl offered her?
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'The fruit was lychee, slightly warm from the grill.'
questions.hint: The narrator names it after tasting it.

questions.prompt: What caused both the narrator and a vendor to end up in tears?
options: A deeply moving conversation about their lives
The smoke from the cooking fires
A misunderstanding that they both found very funny
The vendor offered to give her food for free
questions.explanation: The narrator says she 'laughed so hard at a misunderstanding with a vendor that we both ended up in tears.'
questions.hint: The tears were from laughter, not sadness.

questions.prompt: How long did the narrator spend at the night market?
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'I ended up spending four hours at that market without intending to.'
questions.hint: She notes she stayed much longer than she planned.

questions.prompt: What does the narrator say travel can do, according to the final paragraph?
options: Make you appreciate your home country more
Make you feel utterly lost and completely at home at the same time
Give you confidence to live in a foreign country
Change the way you think about food and culture
questions.explanation: The narrator concludes: 'Travel has a strange power: it can make you feel utterly lost and completely at home at the same time.'
questions.hint: She uses a paradox — two opposite feelings at once.

cloze.template: Nobody had warned me quite how loud it would be. I had stepped off a crowded bus into a narrow street, and suddenly the night market was all around me — a wall of sound, colour, and heat that hit me before I had time to prepare for it. Strings of bare light bulbs hung between the stalls, {{b1}} a warm yellow glow over everything. Generators hummed at the edges. Vendors called out to passing customers in a language I did not understand, but the friendliness in their voices was {{b2}}. The smell was the first thing to really stop me in my tracks. Somewhere nearby, meat was being grilled over charcoal, and the smoke {{b3}} through the air in slow, fragrant waves. Mixed in with it were the sharp scents of fresh herbs, the {{b4}} tang of a sauce I could not identify, and beneath all of that, something sweet — grilled banana, I later discovered. I had been travelling alone for three weeks by that point, and I had grown {{b5}} to navigating unfamiliar places. But nothing had quite prepared me for the {{b6}} of this place — every square metre filled with something to look at, eat, or hear. I worked my way slowly through the crowd, pausing at each stall to see what was on offer. A woman was assembling bowls of noodle soup with extraordinary speed, {{b7}} broth from an enormous pot. An old man sat cross-legged on a low plastic stool, frying something that sizzled loudly in a wok. A teenage girl with a shy smile held out a skewer of fruit for me to try. I {{b8}} for a moment — not because I was afraid, but because I wanted to hold on to that particular feeling: of being handed something by a stranger in a place you do not belong, and knowing that you are welcome anyway. The fruit was lychee, slightly warm from the grill. It was unlike anything I had eaten before. I ended up spending four hours at that market without intending to. I tried things I could not name, pointed at dishes and {{b9}} for the best, and once laughed so hard at a misunderstanding with a vendor that we both ended up in tears. By the time I finally found my way back to the main road, I was full, tired, and oddly {{b10}}. Travel has a strange power: it can make you feel utterly lost and completely at home at the same time. That night market did both, and I was grateful for every overwhelming, glorious minute of it.

blanks.explanation: 'Casting a glow' is a set collocation — light 'casts' a glow rather than 'spreads' or 'throws' one in standard English.
'Unmistakable' means impossible to confuse with anything else — a stronger and more precise word than 'obvious' in this context.
'Drifted through the air' captures the slow, gentle movement of smoke — 'drifted' has a more evocative, atmospheric quality.
'Vinegary tang' is the collocation used — 'vinegary' specifically evokes the smell and taste of vinegar.
'Grown accustomed to' is the exact phrase — it means to have gradually become familiar or comfortable with something.
'Density' refers to how tightly packed things or people are in a space — the narrator is describing how crowded every square metre was.
'Ladling broth' — a ladle is a deep spoon used for serving soup; 'to ladle' means to use one.
'Hesitated' is the exact word used — it implies uncertainty or reluctance before acting, not merely a pause.
'Hoping for the best' is a fixed idiom meaning to proceed without certainty but optimistically.
'Oddly moved' — 'moved' in an emotional sense means deeply affected or touched; 'oddly' emphasises that this feeling was unexpected.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The narrator describes feeling both 'utterly lost and completely at home' at the same time. Is this kind of disorientation actually part of what makes travel valuable? Can you truly appreciate a new place if it feels completely comfortable and familiar from the start?
